tldr.title: Anthropic apologizes for invisible Claude Fable guardrails
tldr.author: rarisma
bullets: Anthropic apologized for injecting hidden safety prompts into Claude, altering model outputs without developer consent or API visibility.
These invisible guardrails triggered unexpected silent failures and degraded performance in production AI systems relying on Claude.
The event highlights the necessity of deterministic API versioning and transparent alignment protocols for enterprise LLM deployment.

tldr.title: Reuse Less Software
tldr.author: icefox
bullets: Minimize third-party dependencies to reduce supply chain vulnerability vectors and mitigate security risks like malicious npm package injections.
Writing simple, domain-specific code is often cheaper over time than maintaining complex integration glue for bloated, generic external libraries.
Limit micro-dependencies to prevent deep dependency trees, speed up CI/CD pipelines, and simplify license compliance audits.

tldr.title: Hundreds of AUR packages attacked by infostealer
tldr.author: vivicat
bullets: More info in Mastodon post: https://gaysex.cloud/notes/andaxow7itfn05x9 List of affected packages: https://gr.ht/aur_pkg_list.txt
Hundreds of AUR packages attacked by infostealer
Trending on lobste.rs/security тАФ 5 points, 0 comments.

tldr.title: Introduction to nixidy - Kubernetes GitOps with nix
tldr.author: granra
bullets: nixidy enables Kubernetes GitOps by using Nix expressions instead of YAML, Helm, or Kustomize for declarative cluster state management.
It leverages the Nix module system to deliver type-safe, dry-run-validated, and highly reproducible Kubernetes manifest generation.
It bridges the gap between Nix package management and standard GitOps engines like ArgoCD or Flux for declarative deployments.

tldr.title: After nearly breaking, NASA's Deep Space Network "worked well" on Artemis II
tldr.author: Stephen Clark
bullets: "Some missions are using more than what their paperwork would say."
After nearly breaking, NASA's Deep Space Network "worked well" on Artemis II
Submitted by Stephen Clark on arstechnica.com.
